data.title: This Is What AI Commitment Looks Like: $392 Billion And Rising
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

There was a moment earlier this year when the market seemed ready to move on from artificial intelligence. The "AI trade" felt saturated. Valuations had stretched. And for many, the story had started to feel stale. Traders wanted a new narrative, something with more torque. But then, the earnings came in for the period ended March 31, 2025.

From Microsoft (MSFT) and Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL) to Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL) and Meta (META), some of the largest companies in the world1 reminded us of a simple truth: AI is not a trade. It's a transition. It's not a sprint - it's a structural shift reshaping technology, capital allocation and ultimately the nature of competition.

Take capital spending. The idea that we were past "peak capex" has been proven wrong, at least based on the current statements from the companies. Microsoft reiterated its plan to grow FY25 capital expenditures by 58%,2 and it did so not with vague ambition but with surgical intent - shifting spend toward short-duration, revenue-linked server infrastructure. Alphabet maintained its $75 billion capex guidance for 2025, more than doubling its historic run rate.3 Amazon, meanwhile, reported capex that beat estimates by 22% as it built up logistics and infrastructure in anticipation of potential tariff fallout.4 Even Meta, often under-discussed in this context, raised its own capex guidance to a range of $64 billion to $72 billion, with the majority going to AI infrastructure to power its core advertising and recommendation engines.5

The Silent Arms Race behind AI: Cloud Capex Goes Parabolic

While headlines obsess over the next new large language model, robotic agents and AI's potential to write your emails or your code, the real story - the one that explains who wins and who gets left behind - is buried in capital expenditures.

Look at this chart, figure 1. It's not just a bar graph. It's the pulse of a new industrial revolution.

From 2013 to 2020, cloud infrastructure capex rose methodically - from $32 billion to $119 billion. That's significant but manageable. Post-2020? The curve steepens. By 2024, we hit $285 billion. And in 2025 alone, the top 11 cloud providers are forecasted to deploy a staggering $392 billion - more than the entire previous two years combined.

That's the story: AI isn't just an algorithmic leap - it's a hardware war. The shift from software-scale AI to infrastructure-scale AI means the bottleneck isn't talent, or model design, or even regulation. It's data centers, power, fiber, cooling and chips.

And this surge in spending isn't discretionary. It's existential. If you're a cloud provider and you don't scale compute, storage and throughput to match AI's appetite, you're out of the game. Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet and their challengers know this. That's why the capex needle is vertical.

Three Key Takeaways for Investors and Strategists

1. Infrastructure is the new alpha. The value chain of AI is collapsing downward. If you're not tracking semiconductor supply, power grid reliability and hyperscaler buildouts, you're missing the picture.

2. Capex is not cyclical here - it's foundational. This isn't like prior waves of tech spending that faded when consumer demand softened. AI workloads - training and inference - are compute-thirsty and persistent.

3. Index exposure may not be enough. Many traditional indexes under-weight the arms suppliers of this war: data center real estate investment trusts (REITs), optical networking firms, custom silicon fabricators and even advanced heating, ventilation and air conditioning (HVAC) companies.

Figure 1: Cloud Capex Expenditures for the 11 Largest Players (2025 Represents an Estimate)

Source: Morgan Stanley Research, "Cloud Capex Tracker: '25 growth now at 38% Y/Y" [Equity research report], 2025. 2025 is shaded in a lighter color because it represents an estimated value, not a completed year, based on what the 11 companies have guided so far. This guidance can change, reducing the 2025 figure. Past performance is not indicative of future results.

Bottom line: This is not maintenance mode. This is a long-term buildout of the scaffolding for the next phase of compute.

AI Is Starting to Make an Impact in the Results

But capital spend is only part of the story. These companies are no longer just investing in AI; they are now generating revenue with it. Microsoft's Azure AI contributed 16 percentage points to Azure's 35% year-over-year growth.6 AWS, Amazon's cloud unit, posted a 39% EBIT margin, crediting GenAI for operational leverage.7 Alphabet's cloud division hit an all-time high 18% operating margin,8 while Meta is embedding generative AI throughout its ad stack to drive personalization, discovery and engagement - all of which reinforce the core revenue engine. Only Apple was conspicuously quiet, offering no new updates on Siri or GenAI monetization, even as its peers continue to sprint forward.

Of Course, the Tariffs…

Yet, just as digital intelligence accelerates, real-world friction is mounting. Tariffs, supply chains and geopolitics are now inseparable from technology strategy. Amazon is modeling a 145% China tariff starting in Q3 2025, already absorbing more than $1 billion in one-time logistics and inventory repositioning costs.9 Apple expects $900 million in tariff-related costs in the June quarter alone, despite shifting over half of its U.S.-bound iPhone assembly to India.10 Alphabet and Meta, while less exposed to hardware flows, are dealing with second-order effects: advertisers trimming budgets, cloud customers pulling back and an underlying uncertainty that can't be hedged with servers.

Everything Builds on Earnings and Cash Flows

In this environment, the quality of cash flow becomes a kind of North Star. Microsoft delivered a 7% EPS beat with operating margin expansion, even as it scaled investment.11 Alphabet posted $2.81 in GAAP EPS, well above estimates, partly aided by an $8 billion equity investment gain but supported by genuine operating leverage.12 Amazon's cash flow turned negative this quarter, a reflection of its pre-tariff inventory bulking.

Capital return activity reinforces this picture. Apple announced $100 billion in share buybacks and a 4% dividend hike.13 Alphabet introduced a new $70 billion buyback authorization and raised its quarterly dividend by 5%.14 Microsoft, as steady as ever, continued returning capital without fanfare. Amazon, true to its growth-first DNA, continues to reinvest.

Strong Businesses, but Also Important Risks

Still, the road ahead is not without risk. Apple must scale its India supply chain before tariffs close in. Alphabet is confronting the possibility that its own Gemini-generated answers may cannibalize search ad clicks, as well as a series of court rulings where possible remedies include suggestions to break up the company. Microsoft sees strong demand but warned of a supply-demand imbalance in AI infrastructure heading into the second half of the year. Amazon needs tariff clarity to offer any meaningful guidance. And Meta, with its ad business still vulnerable to economic cyclicality, is betting that infrastructure and models can offer more resilience than forecasts.

This is where we are: in the middle of a generational technology shift that is increasingly grounded in cash flows, capex strategy, margin discipline and geopolitical navigation. The market may still be trying to treat AI like a theme to be rotated into or out of. But the companies building this future aren't playing that game.

They're laying track for a new digital economy.

And if Q1 2025 reminded us of anything, it's that we are still in the early chapters of that story.

Conclusion: A Diversified Approach Focused on the "Mag 7"15

We have been writing about some of the largest companies in the U.S. market, and they tend to be included within the broader moniker "Magnificent 7" that was coined to account for how basically seven companies were responsible for such a large portion of the U.S. equity market's gains over 2023 and 2024.

The WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth Fund (QGRW) is not explicitly designed to gain exposure to these companies, but in tracking the WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth Index before fees and expenses, the characteristics like return on equity, earnings growth and return on assets that are favored by this approach are also prevalent in these seven large companies. Market capitalization weighting pushes large allocations in their direction once they qualify.

Figure 2: Standardized Performance

Sources: WisdomTree, FactSet, specifically data from the Fund Comparison Tool in the PATH suite of tools, accessed 5/3/25 with returns as of 3/31/25. NAV denotes total return performance at net asset value. MP denotes market price performance. Past performance is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For the most recent month-end and standardized performances, click here.

The ultimate exposure of QGRW elevates the Magnificent 7 companies into the portfolio's defining thesis, which is clear in figure 3. Apple, Nvidia and Microsoft alone comprise more than 30%, reflecting that the AI-powered, platform-dominated economy is a central exposure.

Figure 3: A Picture of the Top 10 Holdings

Sources: WisdomTree, FactSet, specifically the Fund Compare tool from WisdomTree's PATH set of tools, with holdings data as of 4/30/25. Subject to change.

When the market reinterpreted the Trump administration's evolving communications - particularly around tariffs, tax and tech policy - it was portfolios like QGRW, concentrated in the companies the market believes may outrun policy shifts. Over the April 21 to May 16 window, QGRW pulled decisively ahead of the S&P 500, as investors recalibrated toward platforms with pricing power, cash flows and moats that may not be subject to election year whiplash. Still, we recognize that a big part of this move was catalyzed by the seemingly negotiated pause in tariff escalation between the U.S. and China.

Figure 4: The Late-April 2025 U.S. Equity Market Rally

Sources: WisdomTree, FactSet, specifically data from the Fund Comparison Tool in the PATH suite of tools, accessed 5/3/25 with returns as of 4/21/25 to 5/16/25. NAV denotes total return performance at net asset value. MP denotes market price performance. Past performance is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For the most recent month-end and standardized performances, click here.

Of course, that same high-conviction exposure cuts both ways. In the first week of April, as we see in figure 5, when markets briefly recoiled on fears of escalating tariffs and broader risk-off sentiment, QGRW fell harder than the S&P 500 - underscoring what may happen when the market questions the multiple expansion supporting the Magnificent 7.

Figure 5: The Early-April 2025 U.S. Equity Market Downdraft

Sources: WisdomTree, FactSet, specifically data from the Fund Comparison Tool in the PATH suite of tools, accessed 5/3/25 with returns as of 4/2/25 to 4/8/25. NAV denotes total return performance at net asset value. MP denotes market price performance. Past performance is not indicative of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. For the most recent month-end and standardized performances, click here.

Bottom line

While short-term periods can be quite volatile, if investors are looking at some of the world's biggest companies that we have discussed in this piece, QGRW represents an interesting way to consider a diversified exposure that does push an emphasis in their direction. Our opinion is that the longer-term case for bearishness on these companies could be overstated - and we'll continue to watch how things play out.

______________

1 "Largest" is determined on the basis of market capitalization measured as of May 2, 2025, with source Bloomberg.

2 Source: Morgan Stanley Research, "Cloud Capex Tracker: '25 Growth Now At 38% Y/Y" [Equity research report], 5/1/25.

3 Source: Goldman Sachs Global Investment Research, "Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL): Q1'25 review - Results demonstrate resilient search & rising AI narratives" [Equity research report], 4/25/25.

4 Source: Morgan Stanley Research, "Amazon.com Inc: Solid results and strategy - Now we need tariff visibility" [Equity research report], 5/2/25.

5 Source: Morgan Stanley Research, "Cloud Capex Tracker: '25 growth now at 38% Y/Y" [Equity research report], 2025.

6 Source: Morgan Stanley Research, "Microsoft: 3Q25 results - Are we ready to believe?" [Equity research report], 5/1/25.

7 Source: Morgan Stanley Research, "Amazon.com Inc: Solid results and strategy - Now we need tariff visibility" [Equity research report], 2025.

8 Source: Goldman Sachs Global Investment Research, "Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL): Q1'25 review - Results demonstrate resilient search & rising AI narratives" [Equity research report], 2025.

9 Source: Morgan Stanley Research, "Amazon.com Inc: Solid results and strategy - Now we need tariff visibility" [Equity research report], 2025.

10 Source: Morgan Stanley Research, "Apple, Inc.: Some clarity, but plenty of uncertainties" [Equity research report], 2025.

11 Source: Morgan Stanley Research, "Microsoft: 3Q25 results - Are we ready to believe?" [Equity research report], 2025.

14 Source: Goldman Sachs Global Investment Research, "Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL): Q1'25 review - Results demonstrate resilient search & rising AI narratives" [Equity research report], 2025.

15 Short for Magnificent 7, which refers to Apple, Amazon.com, Alphabet, Meta Platforms, Microsoft, Nvidia and Tesla.

Growth stocks, as a group, may be out of favor with the market and underperform value stocks or the overall equity market. Growth stocks are generally more sensitive to market movements than other types of stocks. The Fund is non-diversified; as a result, changes in the market value of a single security could cause greater fluctuations in the value of Fund shares than would occur in a diversified fund. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit. The Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index or take defensive positions in declining markets, and the Index may not perform as intended. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

data.title: Nextracker: Near-Term Outlook Cloudy, Long-Term Prospects Remain Attractive
data.description:
When investing, pessimism is your friend, euphoria the enemy. - Warren Buffett

Since the last time we wrote about Nextracker (NASDAQ:NXT), there have been a number of important developments that merit another evaluation of the company and its prospects. There is currently significant pessimism in the renewable energy sector, as there is speculation that some of the provisions in the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA) could be modified or terminated by the U.S. Congress. Nextracker sounded confident in the most recent shareholder letter saying they expect rationality will prevail, especially given how important the solar industry has become in terms of jobs and investment in many states.

We are hopeful that any changes to the IRA and other relevant energy policy will be done in a fashion that preserves our industry’s ability to meaningfully contribute to meeting our nation’s rapidly growing electricity demand.

Still, potential regulatory changes, coupled with increased tariffs on solar panels is likely to have some impact on Nextracker's business, even if Nextracker has products with 100% U.S. domestic content. This has understandably caused significant pessimism from investors toward companies operating in the solar industry, even those providing complementary products, such as Nextracker, Enphase (ENPH), and SolarEdge (SEDG). Despite the very elevated uncertainty and IRA risks, Nextracker has executed its business plan so well that shares have actually delivered impressive returns year-to-date.

More specifically, Nextracker reported another strong quarter and end to its fiscal year 2025. Healthy bookings helped the company increase the backlog again, making it the ninth consecutive quarter of sequential growth in its backlog. This is one of the main reasons that management sounded very confident during the earnings call about their prospects for this year.

While solar trackers are relatively commoditized, Nextracker has differentiated itself by offering premium quality products, nimble terrain-coping capabilities, resilience-heavy designs like their "Hail Pro" offerings, and the TrueCapture intelligent software.

FY2025 Earnings Results

In the fourth quarter of fiscal year 2025, Nextracker delivered record revenue of $924 million, a 26% increase year-over-year. Revenue for the whole year was $3 billion, adjusted EBITDA grew almost 50% to $776 million, and the backlog now exceeds $4.5 billion.

During the quarter, Nextracker made a small acquisition of an electrical balance of systems (eBOS) business called Bentek, for approximately $78 million. This continues the company's strategy of purchasing adjacent businesses that move Nextracker closer toward being able to offer complete turn-key solar plants.

Software Services

An interesting statistic is that solar trackers can increase energy production by up to 30% compared to fixed-tilt installations. They also increase costs, which is why it is critical to push the energy production increase as much as possible, while lowering tracking costs.

At the end of the day most of Nextracker's customers really care about the levelized cost of electricity (LCOE), which is why squeezing a little bit more energy from the same hardware can be a game changer. This is probably why Nextracker developed its TrueCapture® energy yield management, and NX Navigator™ advanced monitoring and control platforms. TrueCapture is a smart control system focused on optimizing energy yield, while NX Navigator is a broader platform that encompasses TrueCapture and provides additional monitoring, control, and risk management capabilities.

While the revenues that these software solutions generate are relatively small, roughly 2% of total revenue, it is likely that they help differentiate Nextracker's offerings. In fact, the company reported record increasing attach rates.

Financials

Nextracker has been growing revenue and earnings at an impressive pace, despite some deceleration from the ~50% growth rate seen around the time the company began trading. Still, over the past two years the average quarterly year-over-year growth rate has been a very respectable 27%. This has also been profitable growth, as the company has operating margins in excess of 20%, and its earnings per share (EPS) are trending higher. Nextracker also has a very healthy balance sheet, ending the year with over $766 million in cash and basically no long-term debt.

Tariff Impact

It was surprising how confident management sounded during the most recent earnings call that they can successfully navigate the tariff war. While this is understandable to a certain degree given the company has products with 100% U.S. content and several U.S. manufacturing partners that can help the company ramp up production as needed. The company is also relatively insulated from counter-tariffs despite operating in more than 40 countries, given that it has an asset-light manufacturing strategy with over 90 global partner factory facilities.

We are more concerned with the indirect impact from tariffs on complementary items needed for solar plants, mainly solar panels and in some cases batteries. This could result in some projects being cancelled or postponed.

Future Outlook

Guidance for fiscal year 2026 calls for relatively modest growth, but it is understandable that management would want to be prudent given the elevated uncertainty. Revenue is expected to grow approximately 10%, but margins and profitability are expected to be pressured by the tough environment. Still, the record backlog and customers increasingly looking to choose proven solutions mitigates some of the headwinds.

One key assumption used in the company's guidance is that the 45X incentives will continue for the full year. CFO Chuck Boynton provided a good summary of the approach the company took when determining guidance for FY2026, with opposing factors like tariffs and a robust backlog playing a role.

[...] expect structural gross margins for fiscal '26 in the low-30s. This takes into consideration geographic mix, impact of tariffs and the investment to scale the recently announced acquisitions. While we have taken a prudent approach to guidance due to ongoing macroeconomic uncertainty, our confidence in the business is grounded in several key drivers. The strength of our record backlog a continued flight to quality among solar developers and the deep capability and commitment of our global team.

Valuation

Compared to other companies serving the solar industry, such as micro-inverter manufacturers like Enphase and SolarEdge, or direct competitors like Array Technologies (ARRY), Nextracker does trade with a relatively high Price/Sales ratio. Only Enphase Energy currently trades at a higher multiple. Still, this is understandable as only Nextracker and Enphase Energy are currently profitable, although Array Technologies is expected to return to profitability in the coming year.

In any case, for a company that has been growing revenue above 20% per year, a roughly 15x Price/Earning multiple appears very reasonable. If the uncertainty caused by tariffs and IRA incentives was resolved in a positive manner, it is likely that the valuation multiple would expand considerably.

Risks

The biggest risk we see for Nextracker is policy uncertainty, in the form of tariffs and renewable incentives. This is followed by potential pricing and margin pressures, which could be more intense if solar power demand slows down as a result of policy changes. Exchange rates, supply chain, and logistical risks are also significant.

These risks are mitigated by the solid balance sheet, leading market position in most of its markets, brand reputation, technological innovations, and global supply chain strength.

Conclusion

Compared to last time we analyzed Nextracker, we see a cloudier outlook. With shares trading at a higher price despite increased uncertainty, we are adjusting our rating to a 'Hold'. We continue to believe in the company, and are impressed by the superb execution of their business plan, with consistent growth in the backlog and increasing the technical capabilities through organic and inorganic strategies. Despite the reasonable valuation and solid backlog, we believe more caution is warranted. We continue to like the company, and believe it remains one of the most attractive in the solar industry.

data.title: Evergy: A Terrific Utility Pick To Sleep Well At Night
data.description:
This article was coproduced with Kody Kester.

In dividend growth investing, I believe that quality is of the utmost importance. How can it be assessed, though?

Quality can be gauged by a combination of a company's track record as a dividend grower and its forward-looking fundamentals picture. As has been shown by past dividend cuts from former Dividend Kings and Dividend Aristocrats, reputation alone isn't always enough to generate growing passive income. The fundamentals need to support dividend growth for it to be sustained over time.

The regulated electric utility Evergy, Inc. (NASDAQ:EVRG) is a company I believe meets both of these requirements. When I last covered EVRG with a buy rating in April, I liked its increased five-year capital spending plan. The investment-grade balance sheet was another plus. Shares were also discounted enough for the annual total return potential to be in the low-double-digits.

Today, I'm reaffirming my buy rating. EVRG's service territories have ample economic development underway to warrant its five-year capital spending plan. The company's BBB credit rating from S&P makes its capital spending plan achievable. EVRG's shares are also almost as discounted as they were when I previously highlighted it.

Ample Catalysts To Drive Future Growth

On May 8th, EVRG shared its first-quarter earnings report for the period ended March 31st. The company's operating revenue edged 3.3% higher over the year-ago period to $1.37 billion in the quarter. That topped the Seeking Alpha analyst consensus by $357 million during the quarter.

Cold winter weather powered 8.4% and 3.2% respective increases in total retail demand for the residential and commercial customer classes for the first quarter.

That was partially offset by an 8.3% decline in total industrial customer class retail demand in the quarter. The good news is that a large customer's unplanned maintenance shutdown was the primary driver of reduced industrial usage. EVRG anticipates that the customer will be producing near normal levels this month.

Overall, these elements are what led EVRG's total retail electric demand to rise by 2.7% during the quarter.

Shifting gears to the bottom line, the utility's adjusted EPS were flat at $0.54 in the first quarter. That missed the analyst consensus by $0.13 during the quarter. Recovery of regulated investments ($0.13) and favorable weather ($0.01) were equally offset by higher depreciation and amortization and interest expenses ($0.10) and other expenses ($0.04) for the quarter. This is why adjusted EPS remained the same as operating revenue grew in the quarter.

EVRG stood by its expectations of $4.02 in midpoint adjusted EPS for 2025 ($3.92 to $4.12). From the 2024 base of $3.81, this would represent a 5.5% rise. This is also the same as the FAST Graphs analyst consensus, which communicates Wall Street's confidence in company guidance.

What is the reason for this optimism? Strong commercial and industrial load growth is expected in the back half of 2025 (3% to 5% year-over-year for commercial customers and 6% to 8% for industrial customers). That's because Meta Platforms (META) and Panasonic will be ramping up operations to close out the year.

Looking beyond 2025, there are reasons to be bullish. Legislatively, House Bill 2107 was recently signed into law by Gov. Laura Kelly of Kansas. Senate Bill 4 was also signed into law by Gov. Mike Kehoe of Missouri. These laws are both anticipated to be constructive to EVRG's already strong regulatory frameworks and boost infrastructure investments in the region.

The company currently has 1.1 gigawatts of load that is under construction in its service territories. Chairman and CEO David Campbell also pointed out in his opening remarks during the Q1 2025 Earnings Call that 1.3 GW of load is in the final stages of negotiation. These are for two data center projects, with one customer evaluating the Kansas service territory and the other an existing data center customer considering expansion in Missouri.

For these reasons, EVRG plans to invest $17.5 billion between 2025 and 2029. It's forecasted that this will generate an 8.5% annual rate base growth rate over that time.

This is why the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for another 6% adjusted EPS growth in 2026 to $4.26. An additional 6.1% jump in adjusted EPS to $4.52 is the current consensus for 2027.

Financially, EVRG has a few mechanisms it can use to fund this capital spending plan and the $3.6 billion in dividends that are estimated to be paid over that time. The company's operating cash flow is going to fund the majority of this capital spending ($12.5 billion).

The supermajority of the remaining funding will be derived from $5.8 billion in incremental debt issuances. EVRG can use its BBB credit rating from S&P on a stable outlook to cheaply fund this aspect of the capital spending plan. The remaining $2.8 billion of capital will come from equity issuances (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all details in this subhead were according to EVRG's Q1 2025 Earnings Press Release, EVRG's Q1 2025 10-Q Filing, and EVRG's Q1 2025 Earnings Presentation).

Shares Are Still Undervalued

Since my previous article, EVRG's shares have gained 4% to the 9% gains of the S&P 500 index (SP500). Overall, accounting for a slight uptick in the forward 12-month adjusted EPS input, shares are almost as attractive as they were six weeks ago. According to data from FAST Graphs, EVRG's current year P/E ratio of 16.9 is meaningfully lower than the 10-year average P/E ratio of 19.5.

In the years to come, I continue to argue that a fair value P/E ratio of 17.5 is a sensible fair value estimate for the electric utility. That would be one standard deviation below the 10-year average P/E ratio. EVRG's 5.9% annual adjusted EPS growth consensus over the next few years is similar to the 10-year annual adjusted EPS growth rate of approximately 5%.

However, my base case is that 10-year U.S. Treasury (US10Y) yields will remain above the 10-year average of 2.5% per Macrotrends. I believe the 10-year yield will come down to around 3.5% in the years ahead. The investment spread between the average 10-year yield and average 10-year P/E ratio for EVRG was around 250 basis points. Plugging in a similar (225 basis point spread), this is also how I come out to a fair value P/E ratio of 17.5.

The calendar year 2025 is approximately 38% complete. That suggests another 62% of 2025 and 38% of 2026 lie ahead over the coming 12 months. This yields a forward 12-month adjusted EPS input of $4.11.

Applying a 17.5 P/E ratio to this adjusted EPS input, I get a fair value of $72 a share. Compared to the current $68 share price (as of May 20th, 2025), that equates to a 6% discount to fair value. If EVRG meets growth expectations and returns to fair value, it could have a 30% cumulative total return ahead by the end of 2027.

More Dividend Growth Is On The Way

EVRG's 4% forward dividend yield comes in moderately higher than the utilities sector's median forward yield of 3.3%. That's enough for Seeking Alpha's Quant System to issue B+ and B grades for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield, respectively.

EVRG's above-average starting income also appears to be relatively secure. The electric utility's adjusted EPS payout ratio is positioned to be in the mid-60% range for 2025. This would be firmly within its targeted payout ratio of 60% to 70%. According to The Dividend Kings' Zen Research Terminal, EVRG's payout ratio is also better than the 75% payout ratio that rating agencies like to see from the industry. That garners a B+ grade from the Quant System for overall dividend safety.

This puts EVRG in a position where it can keep delivering respectable dividend growth to shareholders. In the past five years, the electric utility's dividend has compounded by around 6% annually. This was above the sector median of 5.2%. The Quant System anticipates annual forward dividend per share growth of 4.5%.

In my view, this is probably about 50 basis points too conservative. That's because EVRG is reasonably confident that it can grow adjusted EPS by the top half of its 4% to 6% annual target. The payout ratio is also reasonable enough that dividend growth should track in line with adjusted EPS growth in the coming years. As a result, the Quant System awards a B grade for overall dividend growth.

EVRG has grown its dividend for 21 consecutive years. This is well above the sector median of 10.3 years and implies that the utility remains on track to become a Dividend Aristocrat in 2029. That's why EVRG enjoys an A+ grade from the Quant System for overall dividend consistency.

Risks To Consider

EVRG is a fundamentally sound regulated utility. Yet, it still faces risks that could derail or even shatter the investment thesis.

As I have noted in past articles, one key risk to EVRG remains its geographic concentration. The electric utility is completely based in Kansas and Missouri. If either of these two markets slumps demographically or economically, that could hurt future demand for EVRG's electric utility services. From my perspective, the probability of this is low given that both states are becoming more attractive to new advanced manufacturing and data center projects. However, it's still worth watching.

Additionally, EVRG's concentration opens it to the potential for unfavorable rate case outcomes. Such an outcome in either of these markets could prompt rating agencies to issue credit rating downgrades. This would make it more difficult for EVR to execute its capital spending plan and likely impact the company's growth prospects.

Regarding natural disasters, both Kansas and Missouri are especially prone to tornadoes. The average annual number of tornadoes for each state from 2003 through 2022 was 87 and 53, respectively. An especially bad year for tornadoes could result in damage inflicted on EVRG's infrastructure exceeding commercial insurance coverage. In a worst-case scenario, that could harm its earnings power.

One last risk to EVRG is the potential for successful cyber breaches. If severe enough, such an event could cost the company billions of dollars via customer lawsuits and regulatory penalties.

Summary: A Utility Worth Considering

EVRG is a utility with plenty of tailwinds working in its favor. The company has the balance sheet to support its five-year capital spending plans. EVRG's dividend is also well-covered and poised for more mid-single-digit annual percentage growth.

On the valuation front, shares also look to be modestly discounted. Put it all together, and EVRG could be set up for double-digit annual total returns for the foreseeable future. That's why I'm maintaining my buy rating.

data.title: Kaspi: This 7x P/E Fintech Super-App Could Be Your Portfolio's Energizer
data.description:
Introduction

The Kazakh stock market-the market cap sum of all of its publicly traded firms-is less than PayPal Holdings, Inc. (PYPL), the 150th component of the S&P 500 index (by market cap). And it's unsurprising, as just ~20 million people populate Kazakhstan, the 9th biggest country in the world, where Chevron (CVX) produces a lot of crude oil.

Anyway, the relative smallness of this stock market doesn't mean that investors can't find great growth opportunities there. For example, when I first covered Freedom Holding Corp. (FRHC) in late May 2021, the stock was trading at ~$50 apiece, and it has managed to gain over 250% in nominal terms since then. Since my latest update in mid-October 2024, FRHC has been up 76.85% while the S&P 500 index (SPX, SP500, SPY) has been basically flat for the same period.

But today's article is dedicated to a direct peer of Freedom Holding-Joint Stock Company Kaspi.kz (OTC:KSPI), which, I believe, has a tremendous expansion potential in the next few years, thanks to the strong tailwinds of the addressable markets, their unique market proposition, and a well-structured business ecosystem. At the same time, following KSPI's recent dip, the stock has become a real bargain, which pays a hefty dividend amid a massive buyback program, trading at less than 7x forward earnings.

My Investment Thesis

I sincerely believe that KSPI stock might be the best addition to your GARP portfolio if you're seeking to diversify it with non-U.S. picks. So, I initiated my first "Strong Buy" rating on Kaspi, expecting a massive run-up in price as it attracts investors' attention with its tempting metrics.

The Region Overview

Let's begin with the general region overview. Kazakhstan itself is the largest economy in Central Asia. Located right between Russia and China, the country holds ~1.7% of the world's proven oil reserves and about 1.2% of its natural gas reserves, according to UBS data (proprietary source, May 12th, 2025).

Kazakhstan is the world's largest producer of uranium and ranks in the top ten countries for coal, gold, chrome, zinc, lead, and bauxite reserves in the world. So, in recent years, the country tried to diversify its materials-heavy economy structure through the transportation and logistics sector's increased trade volume via the Trans-Caspian International Transport Route (Middle Corridor), which connects Europe and Asia while bypassing Russia, the hydrocarbon sector is contributing >10% to the country's GDP, accounting for half of its exports, and ~30% of its fiscal revenues (based on UBS's proprietary data).

According to the IMF, Kazakhstan's fiscal and external deficits are expected to remain modest at around 3-4% of GDP in 2025, despite some widening from around 1.5% in 2024. The country has quite low public debt (~24% of GDP) and ample sovereign assets (~21% of GDP), so its stable Baa1 rating from Moody's is likely to stay there for the next few years unless we see something unusually bad on the geopolitical landscape.

Kaspi's Overview

While Kazakhstan's economy is centered around material extraction, the banking and e-commerce sectors are flourishing as well. For example, Statista projects the e-commerce market there to reach ~$3.56 billion this year and keep expanding by a CAGR of 10.12% over the next few years. The corresponding CAGR in, say, the U.S., is 8.22% because of the higher user penetration.

Kaspi.kz started off as a common commercial bank (initially Bank Kaspiyskiy), but it eventually changed to a major player in multiple addressable markets such as digital banking, fintech, e-commerce, e-government services, delivery, etc. Think of Chinese WeChat, but without that messaging and social media app supremacy (Kaspi's core business is more heavily weighted towards transactions and service provision).

Kaspi rode the wave of digitalization of banking services in Kazakhstan. If back in 2017, about 54% of people in Kazakhstan had made a payment online, in 2021, we saw that figure rise to 78%. In 2024, cashless transactions exceeded 85% last year, according to the National Bank's annual report. The previously existing low base user penetration really helped Kaspi to establish itself as a reliable payments and transactions provider during online shopping: as of the latest data, ~70% of Kazakhstan's population uses Kaspi.kz's super app in some way, according to W Fintechs' newsletter.

With the user perspective market saturated, Kaspi.kz aims to be central in how people buy and pay for goods, as well as manage their personal finances. Out of 2 million small and medium-sized merchants, 580,000 of them accept payments from Kaspi.kz. The company has benefited from several favorable winds in recent decades.

The population of Kazakhstan Republic is currently ~20.8 million, and Kaspi reports ~14.9 million monthly active users on its official IR website, with 68% of this MAU comprising the "daily active users" metric. Just to make sure one understands the significance of this DAU/MAU relation: Amazon's (AMZN) DAU/MAU is ~32% (~98 million DAU divided by 310 million MAU, rough estimates). Kaspi's capabilities of monetizing the marketplace it operates look tremendous as they make money on each cycle and turn off their users' journey.

Kaspi has 3 operating segments: Fintech (₸ 343 billion in Q1 2025, +18% YoY), Marketplace (₸ 201 billion, +33% YoY), and Payments (₸ 147 billion, +16%). As you can see, their top line is expanding rapidly, so as a result, the consolidated revenue of ₸ 821.8 billion (roughly ~$1.61 billion at the current USD/KZT exchange rate) was up ~48.66% YoY in Q1 FY2025. The margins shrank in Q1 because of the growth in e-Grocery, which is a newer and rapidly scaling 1P (first-party) business that involved higher initial investment in logistics, warehousing, and so on. Also, a major decline was seen in their recently bought Hepsiburada business, which reported a hefty drop in EBITDA last quarter due to "negative operational gearing" and "growth in loan loss provisions".

The firm hasn't managed to meet the Street's EPS consensus, although they beat the top line, and generally, their EPS showed a YoY growth of 14.77% (in tenge current).

Although that was clearly a temporary phenomenon-I mean, the growth in loan loss provisions and an overall boost in investments for e-Grocery during the period-the market didn't like Q1 and punished KSPI. The contrast is particularly seen when we match its YTD performance with FRHC's:

But unlike Freedom Holding, Kaspi's business seems much better diversified outside just banking and financials. FRHC is known primarily for its vast brokerage services, and it doesn't have that breadth in e-commerce (although it tries hard to expand on that front as well, seeing Kaspi's successes).

Regarding both firms' main activity — commercial banking — the market conditions in Kazakhstan are becoming a bit more complicated than before. Kaspi's CFO mentioned during the earnings call that they anticipate a new 10% tax on revenue from investments in government securities and an increase in National Bank reserve requirements-all that together might impact KSPI's net income by ~200 bps this year, which is another negative factor driving Kaspi stock's post-earnings dip.

Anyway, as Kaspi reached its near-maximum penetration levels in Kazakhstan, it's now seemingly trying to expand in Turkey-the Q1 loss from the Hepsiburada business isn't stopping the management. In March 2025, they agreed to acquire Rabobank's Turkey division.

So, knowing Kaspi's approach in Kazakhstan, I believe they're going to make a similarly structured super app, but this time in Turkey, where their Hepsiburada business already has at least 12.3 million active users. As of note, it's just less than 15% of Turkey's total population, so the user base for continued growth in that region is much more massive compared to Kaspi's local market, in my understanding.

While the management also noted on the call that "potential macro uncertainty might impact "higher ticket verticals" like consumer electronics and cars," hence disproportionately affecting their GMV, I don't actually see that. The Q1 data would have shown the initial GMV hit, but outside of the regulatory-driven demand shock in one category (smartphones), I don't see anything I could be worried about right away.

Kaspi's Mispricing Explanation

Kaspi's operating income has been growing at a CAGR of ~33% over the past decade, with diluted EPS going from $0.08 in FY2026 to $11.10 on a TTM basis (I'm afraid to spell out this growth rate). Yes, the local market in Kazakhstan is clearly becoming more and more penetrated, and the banking sector in general has become more crowded with alike tech solutions (primarily thanks to Freedom's efforts in recent years). But nobody cancels out Kaspi's growth prospects: the current Wall Street consensus anticipates that the firm's EPS will grow from $12.30 in FY2025 to $17.51 by FY2027 (3-year CAGR of ~19% if my math is right.

If the current stock price stays constant, its price-to-earnings multiple will effectively come to 4.62x. Let me repeat it again: Kaspi is a fintech firm, it's not a highly cyclical shipping dry bulking company, whose revenues and EBITDA might drop like a rock tomorrow morning. Some projects and business parts like Hepsiburada do show volatile results, but Kaspi is the number one fintech and marketplace provider in its region; its business is well-diversified to tolerate fluctuations in high-growth niches and ventures.

Western investors are used to the fact that emerging market stocks tend to trade much cheaper than their U.S. peers. But when we look at Kaspi, we see a massive undervaluation, especially when we match its forwarding P/E with its forwarding EPS growth rate. KSPI is currently 6x chapter than the median of the Financials sector, according to Seeking Alpha:

At the same time, Kaspi used to buy back its stock from the market: During Q1 2024, they spent ~$6.4 million on repurchases (which is ~0.17% of the entire market cap when annualized) and paid out over $1.2 billion in dividends last year (which is effectively a dividend yield of ~8%). In Q1 FY2025, KSPI hasn't spent a dime on any of these 2 activities, based on its audited financials, but I think they'll restart their generous shareholders' policy again as the initial growth investments in Turkey are done.

So, the current mispricing of KSPI stock isn't that hard to explain: Due to its conservative guidance for FY2025 as a whole and one-off (in my understanding) Hepsiburada business deterioration, the market decided to punish KSPI despite its overall good-looking operating metrics. Add to this the thin liquidity in its stock on the NASDAQ exchange, and also the absence of buybacks and dividends during Q1 FY2025, and you'll get 2 other layers to the explanation.

But once the one-offs are gone, which, I believe, should happen during 2H 2025, the stock's undervaluation should attract those who left their long positions, and the long-term rally will likely continue further.

Risks To Consider

Of course, long ideas like this aren't immune to specific risks, most of which come from the above paragraph explaining the existing mispricing.

On the top macro level, investors should be aware that Kazakhstan and its credit profile are mainly exposed to geopolitical risks from the Russia-Ukraine war. Some Russian entities try to overcome the sanctions by exploiting Kazakhstan's laws, and although the Kazakh government seems to react immediately, it can't entirely ensure the absence of such activities. Also, high trade dependence on Russia (~10% of exports, ~30% of imports) leaves Kazakhstan vulnerable to secondary sanctions. In addition to that, regarding macro, UBS wrote in its note that there's a risk that any major damage to hydrocarbon infrastructure or a sharp drop in oil prices could weaken the country's fiscal and external balances.

When it comes to Kaspi itself, I see a clear risk that if the management's updated guidance for FY2025 turns out to be just the beginning (meaning that GMV takes a serious hit, or the new regulations keep pressing the bottom line), then KSPI's EPS growth will likely stall. Also, FRHC's recent achievement in building out its own ecosystem and the resulting success in top-line expansion are already eating away at a major part of Kaspi's market pie. If these factors intensify, the current FWD P/E of 5.2x might turn into 10x+ over time, so the coming market's repricing might cancel out any chances for a stock price reversal.

Your Takeaway

Despite the above-mentioned risks, when it comes to picking an ideal international candidate to diversify one's mainly U.S.-tilted portfolio, I think Kaspi is one of the best stocks out there.

Yes, there are plenty of reasons why KSPI declined so much on a YTD basis while its main peer outperformed most of the Magnificent 7 firms, but I see even more reasons to stay bullish.

The main catalyst might come in 2H 2025 when we see Kaspi's progress with their Turkey operations, and once they bring in more clarity on shareholders' returns, which is going to be a real game changer.

I see a tremendous opportunity in Kaspi's expansion in the next few years, and while it can get cheaper for sure in the short term, the current lag behind Western peers in terms of P/E, PEG, EV/EBITDA (whatever valuation multiple you choose) leaves a lot of margin of safety.

I initiate my coverage on KSPI with a "Strong Buy" rating.

Good luck with your investments!

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best International Stock Idea investment competition, which runs through June 6. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: The Trade Desk: Business Momentum Ahead For This Category Leader
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Trade Desk (NASDAQ:TTD) is a fast-growing ad tech provider leading the market in demand side programmatic advertising. Their unique selling proposition is absolute transparency in being aligned with the ad buyers, as they are not conflicted by operating a sell side or publishing business. This has been a key reason for The Trade Desk’s success to date, but we will see below that the competitive environment may be changing that could accelerate market share gains.

The Trade Desk’s platform connects ad buyers with content publishers of all forms such as digital display, mobile, and audio, but the largest and fastest growing opportunity is in connected TV, where video ads are shown before, during and after a video is streamed on the internet. Think free streaming providers such as Roku or Tubi, or ad-supported tiers of streaming providers such as Netflix. It remains early days in the streaming industry, as there is a long way to go as traditional linear TV still commands a decent share of TV watch time, but this is declining every year.

TTD is also under-earning, as it is investing heavily in both R&D and sales and marketing, which is depressing earnings and margins in the short term to grow market share. It also has the effect of inflating the multiples the market pays, as the market looks forward to larger future cash flows. I will discuss TTD multiples later in this article, but it is important to remember that these multiples may seem high, but only if you look very short term.

All of this combines into a high-quality company that is a market leader and is about to benefit from a more favorable regulatory and competitive environment that will all help continue to increase sales and expand margins. In this article, I’ll expand on all of the above and discuss why the opportunity exists today.

Business Overview

Programmatic advertising refers to the process where an ad placement on the web or web-enabled service is placed in close to real time and is placed based on the outcome of a real-time auction as the content is loaded. This technology has been around since 2000, but more recently it has been TTD that has pioneered its evolution.

The technology is applied to any online advertising media such as streaming, displays ads, mobile ads, and more recently, audio (think ads on Spotify). The Trade Desk’s platform enables advertisers to create, deploy and track campaigns that are aligned in branding and messaging across all major ad media forms. The software will bid into the vast array of available placements (referred to as inventory), made available by supply side platforms, and can be highly tailored to the advertiser's specifications. The advertiser defines the factors and outcomes that are most important to their campaign and The Trade Desk software (called Kokai, which is replacing the last iteration called Solimar) will analyze the opportunities and only bid where impressions are optimized. This analysis is based on incredible amounts of real-world data and provides highly targeted insights that enhance the yield of a given advertising campaign. This results in better campaign performance and is core to the investment thesis.

Background to the current quarter’s market reaction

The Trade has recently experienced severe share price volatility (both up and down, and both in response to the two most recent quarterly earnings, respectively). Last quarter (Q4'24), The Trade Desk experienced their first earnings miss as a listed company. They noted that they were recalibrating and reorganizing the company to reduce complexity as the company grew. CEO Jeff Green emphasized the fact that they reorganize every year, but this one was bigger than usual due to the immense opportunity set in front of them at present - bigger than they had previously anticipated. TTD increased their resourcing to brands in response to demands from agencies to be more data-driven. Data is TTD’s sweet spot, so they are well-placed to address this need, however, the additional resourcing puts pressure on margins in the short term, even though it speaks to positive momentum in the long term.

It wasn’t a huge miss, and the presented numbers were still objectively good (revenue up 22% y/y, Adjusted EBITDA up 23% y/y and EPS up 85%, 10% above consensus), but the market is more accustomed to TTD beating guidance than missing, even if it was only by 2% at the revenue line and 3% at the EBITDA line.

Worse, however, was the Q2'25 guidance, which was for EBITDA to be 24% lower than the consensus expectation on near flat sales. This also led consensus to downgrade earnings for future years. The Trade Desk was trading on over 60x P/E, so the result was an absolute savaging of the share price, falling more than 60%.

Q1’25 Result better than expected

To understand how good this result is, it is necessary to understand where expectations were before TTD downgraded in February.

Before the Q4/2024 release in February, FactSet consensus for Revenue was $582m. Management then tempered this to a guide of $575m and proceeded to report $616m. Similarly, before the downgrade, consensus for Adjusted EBITDA (which excludes stock-based compensation) was $191m. TTD then guided to $145m for the first quarter but reported an astonishing $208m. The below table expands the one above to show how much the Q1'25 report beat the original expectations.

This can be seen visually on the below chart from FactSet that tracks analyst expectations over time.

Regulatory intervention, a tailwind

CEO Jeff Green likes to talk about the competition. In particular, he speaks about what he calls the “wall gardens” frequently. These walled gardens are those large advertising firms that provide a platform for ad placements primarily on their own content, such as Google (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META). To advertise in this environment is opaque and uncompetitive because the walled gardens play for both sides, and in some cases (particularly Amazon) they may even compete with the advertiser in their service offering.

The Department of Justice (DOJ) recently won 2 court cases against Google regarding their advertising business, declaring them an illegal monopoly. The DOJ also recently filed suit against Meta Platforms for a similar complaint. In both cases, the complaint was concerning auction mechanics that benefit Google and Meta at the expense of advertisers and publishers. That little summary is sufficient for our purposes, but Jeff Green’s commentary is worth reading in full if you’re interested:

Green has said in previous conference calls that he thinks in Google’s case they will do one of two things: they will either divest their network advertising business (the now comparatively small part of their business that competes with the Trade Desk), or have their DSP just focus on their own content publishing platforms such as YouTube, thereby exiting the “open internet” altogether. This would leave a large hole in the industry for established players like TTD to step into.

So Google, Meta, and more recently, Amazon, have been playing an unfair game, bending the rules to their own advantage, and in this environment, The Trade Desk has still been able to thrive and build a $2.5 billion revenue business. What could they achieve on an even playing field? This is the bull case for TTD. Green stated that Google is already winding down some of their unfair practices, and he believes Amazon will be next.

Outlook

As usual, TTD gave guidance for the coming quarter. However, I think it is worth noting that last quarter should give investors pause when it comes to accepting The Trade Desk’s guidance. I am not suggesting any deception; far from it. However, I think such strong results compared to even management’s expectation suggests that the company doesn’t have the clear visibility to earnings that investors may think it does.

The following table demonstrates this clearly: sometimes guidance is on point, but other times they blow it out of the water. Over the last 3 years, quarterly Adjusted EBITDA guidance, has been more than 5% out (mostly to the upside) in 9 out of 11 observations and above 15% in 4 of these. In fact, if you look at the price impact column, the market has actually become accustomed to TTD beating their guide handily, because in most cases if they don’t beat their guide by 10% the stock will fall. This also explains why the post Q4'24 share price reaction was so savage because not only did the result not beat the guidance by 10%+ but it was below the guide, and it downgraded the following quarter.

For the 2Q25 quarter, TTD expects Revenue of more than $682m and Adjusted EBITDA of $259m. Interestingly, this was only slightly higher than consensus expectations of $256m and $680m respectively, telling you that the aggressive buying post report was due to the current period result, not so much the guidance.

Neutral view on Macro environment

One of my main concerns with an investment in TTD has been its exposure to advertising. TTD takes a percentage of gross advertising spend as revenue, so the company is highly leveraged to this part of the economy. In an economic downturn, advertising spend tends to decline, as companies cut costs. It follows, then, that in an economic downturn, TTD’s revenues would also be hit. This should be a key understanding for any investor in the business, and, in my view, is the largest risk to the business.

However, The Trade Desk has noted that they tend to outperform peers in times of economic stress, taking market share because their software is so targeted and because their reporting is so detailed, they can make advertising dollars go further. In times of economic stress, advertising doesn’t go to zero and all people, not just advertisers, tend to look for better value, which is exactly what TTD offers.

So in a downturn, the industry may decline cyclically, TTD may even decline in terms of comparable sales, but it may also present an opportunity for them to add new logos and expand their client base, ready for when the economic turns positive again.

Industry Size and Market Share

Advertising is an enormous industry. One estimate puts the size of the industry at $677 billion last year. Of that, the demand side platform is much smaller, estimated at $75 billion in 2024, but growing much faster than the industry as a whole, at 13% per annum.

The Trade Desk made $2.4 billion revenue in 2024 for about 3.2% market share of the DSP industry. Their revenue growth has been well above 20% in recent years, which demonstrates the rapid increase in market share over this period. More importantly, Seeking Alpha's revenue estimates are for 17% over the next 10 years, implying further market share gains are ahead for The Trade Desk.

I think this is most likely going to come from the Walled Gardens, as discussed above, which is a continuation of what is already occurring. To demonstrate, in 2021, Alphabet's Network business (their open internet DSP advertising business) took in $9.31 billion and was 12% of Group revenue. This has declined in each subsequent year such that in 2024, Network revenue was $7.95 billion, contributing only 8% to Group Revenues. This is clearly not a priority for Alphabet, and the antitrust hit they have taken is likely to accelerate this.

Valuation Undemanding, Business outlook unchanged

The stock price crash that occurred earlier this year was a result of a shift in sentiment that led to severe multiple contraction.

The forward P/E peaked at 84x in December, and was 75x immediately before the Q4 report was released. It then bottomed at about 25x early April and has now – post Q1 release – to around 40x.

In my view, the Q4 report and commentary was a blip and the business case and outlook has not changed. The evidence for this is the Q1 report itself, which, as discussed above, came in ahead of pre-downgrade expectations. We are back to business as usual. Does this mean TTD should go back to trade above 80x P/E? I don’t know, but probably not.

But there is a strong argument to suggest that 40x is still too low: it is close to the lowest it has been since listing and lower than at any time in the last 2 years pre-downgrade. I offer these 2 time periods because the interest rate environment was very different until 2021, but reasonably comparable over the last 2 years.

To put some numbers around this, TTD did 25% revenue growth in Q1 and is guiding to 17% revenue growth in Q2. Seeking Alpha consensus has full-year revenue growth at just 17%, which would be significantly below each of the last 5 years.

It also implies that Q3 and Q4 – mathematically speaking – are expected to be below 17%. I think this is unlikely given the strength of the business and the tailwinds discussed in this article. If we assume the following:

  • 21% revenue growth (something of a middle ground and yet still below recent years),

  • Adjusted net income margin remains steady at 33.8%, and

  • Apply the current forward P/E of 40x to the stock

then The Trade Desk could move to $86.75 at the end of the year. Not too bad, but I don’t think this goes far enough. 43x is a level TTD has barely seen because of its growth and return potential. I think more mean reversion is likely and while its average forward P/E since listing in 2016 of 72x is unlikely, the last 3 years – a time in which interest rates have been higher than previously but steady – may be a better comparison. The P/E range in the last 2 years is between 84x and 25x, but if we apply the mean of this period of 60x, I think this serves as a place where TTD could potentially return to with renewed momentum and confidence in the story. It may take 6 to 12 months to play out, but historically speaking, the potential is there.

Rather than provide a concrete price target or valuation, I present a price guide range. Over the next 12 months, based on full year 2025 earnings, we could see TTD trade at between $86 and $121 depending on what multiple the story attracts. But either way, it provides an attractive entry point at today's price of around $76.

Market Review

Risk assets struggled given uncertainty as President Trump’s administration spurred uncertainty and risk market volatility relating to its tariff policies.

The administration implemented additional 25% tariffs on Canada and Mexico (with Canadian energy an exception at 10%). However, policymakers quickly put the tariffs on a 30-day pause. After the pause, the administration implemented the tariffs but subsequently amended them to exempt imports satisfying United States–Mexico–Canada Agreement (USMCA) rules of origin requirements. The administration also enforced additional 10% tariffs on China and 25% blanket tariffs on steel and aluminum imports from most countries (amending tariffs implemented in 2018). This prompted retaliations from Canada, China and the European Union (EU). The administration also announced 25% tariffs on autos and 25% tariffs on those buying oil from Venezuela, to take effect in April.

The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) announced a slowdown in “quantitative tightening” (QT). From April it will allow a limit of $5bn of Treasuries per month to “roll off” its balance sheet, down from $25bn. It left its cap on agency Mortgage-backed securities (MBS) unchanged at $35bn. The central bank’s quarterly summary of economic projections reflected higher inflation and lower growth forecasts for 2025, with tariffs a major factor. It forecast headline and core inflation at 2.7% and 2.8%, respectively at the end of this year, both up from its 2.5% forecast in December. It also projected Gross Domestic Product (GDP) at 1.7% this year, down from its previous 2.1% forecast. However, the committee made no changes to its median “dot plot” Fed funds rate projections.

Inflation was relatively stable over the period. Headline Consumer price index (CPI) rose slightly from 2.7% to 2.8% and core CPI fell from 3.3% to 3.1%. PCE remained at 2.5% while Core Personal consumption expenditures (PCE) remained at 2.8%. The data did, however, show some encouraging progress in stubborn core services sectors, indicating continued disinflationary momentum ahead of tariff impacts. Consumer confidence deteriorated, however, with the University of Michigan and Conference Board’s consumer survey reporting consumer 1-year and 5-year inflation expectations at 5% and 4.1% per annum (pa) respectively.

The labor market was also relatively stable, with the 6-month rolling average of monthly payroll gains trending at ~180,000. The household survey was weaker, however, with employment falling 588,000. The unemployment rate fell from 4.2% to 4.1%. Notably, however, the U6 underemployment rate (a broader measure that includes those that have stopped looking for work and involuntary part-time workers) rose from 7.7% to 8%, the highest since October 2021.

Equity markets fell sharply, with the S&P 500 and Magnificent 7 indices delivering 4.3% and -16% total returns respectively. Treasury yields fell by up to 43bp across the curve in a flight to quality move. The Bloomberg US Corporate Investment Grade index and the Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield index delivered excess returns of -0.85% and -1.13% respective. However, on a total return basis both indices still ended the quarter in positive territory, at 2.31% and 1% respective, driven by contributions from duration exposure and coupon income.

Performance Review

Underperformance was primarily due to the fund's overweight allocation to risk assets, specifically high yield and investment grade credit. The main positioning change involved adding modest yield and optimizing security selection.

Duration and yield curve positioning was a slight positive during the period. The fund entered the period with a curve steepening bias, which contributed negatively versus the benchmark as the US Treasury curve flattened, albeit we achieved alpha from the overall long duration bias within the fund. There were relative value trades within certain non-US rates which were a modest detractor from performance. However, we took profits on other rates trades, including our euro inflation overweight, short positions in Japanese government bonds, and short positions in Canada and Sweden versus Australia. We maintained the fund’s overweight position in US 5- year duration and UK 30-year duration.

From a sector perspective, the fund’s energy overweight underperformed, as did the auto, and utility overweights. Security selection had a minor negative impact, mainly owing to Agency MBS coupon positioning. We adjusted the fund’s agency MBS allocation to focus on higher coupon exposure, a more defensive posture with respect to interest rate volatility. During January and February, the fund increased its exposure to corporate credit by approximately 2%, focusing on high yield and US domestic tickers. This included a modest but selective increase in exposure to domestic energy and cyclical sectors, such as autos, while decreasing exposure to transportation, financial, and non-cyclical sectors.

Market Outlook

Politics will remain uncertain, but most economists believe tariffs will result in higher near-term inflation and cause a drag on growth. Overall, however, we remain cautiously positive on investment grade credit. Spread repricing, relatively high absolute yields and the contractual nature of may continue to draw investors towards credit markets given equity volatility and valuations.

We expect issuance to remain elevated through the first half of 2025 as corporate treasurers seek to take advantage of demand. This could create attractive opportunities to add exposure cheaply. A significant driver of returns might be the extent the headwind to growth from tariffs could be offset by interest rates. We expect politically induced volatility to offer significant opportunities for active managers, particularly those focused on security and sector selection as well as those able to take a global approach.

Risks

Bonds are subject to interest-rate, credit, liquidity, call and market risks, to varying degrees. Generally, all other factors being equal, bond prices are inversely related to interest-rate changes and rate increases can cause price declines. The use of derivatives involves risks different from, or possibly greater than, the risks associated with investing directly in the underlying assets. Derivatives can be highly volatile, illiquid, and difficult to value and there is the risk that changes in the value of a derivative held by the portfolio will not correlate with the underlying instruments or the portfolio’s other investments. Investing in foreign denominated and/or domiciled securities involves special risks, including changes in currency exchange rates, political, economic, and social instability, limited company information, differing auditing and legal standards, and less market liquidity. These risks generally are greater with emerging-market countries. High yield bonds involve increased credit and liquidity risk than higher-rated bonds and are considered speculative in terms of the issuer’s ability to pay interest and repay principal on a timely basis. Mortgage-backed securities: Ginnie Maes and other securities backed by the full faith and credit of the United States government are guaranteed only as to the timely payment of interest and principal when held to maturity. The market prices for such securities are not guaranteed and will fluctuate. Privately issued mortgage-related securities also are subject to credit risks associated with the underlying mortgage properties. These securities may be more volatile and less liquid than more traditional, government-backed debt securities.
